What does this PR do?
Starting [[c26f7ea](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/commit/c26f7eabf4a1cd25353f22734f439505fe636a45)](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/commit/c26f7eabf4a1cd25353f22734f439505fe636a45), netcdf-c exposes the `NC_FillValue` macro instead of `_FillValue` to avoid collisions with [[C++ standard library](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/issues/2858)](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/issues/2858). However, the previous macro, `_FillValue`, was fully removed causing netcdf-c consumers, including GDAL, fail to build.

It's unlikely that this naming change will be backported to the previous netcdf-c releases, so we have to account for both macros variants. We do so by introducing our own macro, `NCDF_FillValue`, and using that in places where `_FillValue` was previously used. If `NC_FillValue` is defined by `netcdf.h`, `NCDF_FillValue` expands to it and, if it's not, to `_FillValue`.

I was able to successfully build the GDAL against netcdf-c both with:
1. `_FillValue`: v4.9.2 (from GitHub sources, Amazon Linux 2023's dnf, and macOS homebrew)
2. `NC_FillValue`: v4.9.3dev (the latest commit from the `main`, This issue is caused by the ongoing cleanup of C++ standard library from transitive includes, if they aren't explicitely allowed by the standard.

In particular, in [string_fastsearch.h](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/9e43697124aa7b854f6515b189233286f6a305d7/numpy/_core/src/umath/string_fastsearch.h#L132) we rely on `std::ptrdiff_t` but don't explicitely include its header, `cstddef`, as prescribed bt the [standard](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/ptrdiff_t).

Before C++23, this worked fine as some other headers we included transitvely included `cstddef`. 

Without this fix, both on Clang/LLVM and GCC upstreams the following error is observed:
`../numpy/_core/src/umath/string_fastsearch.h:132:5: error: no type named 'ptrdiff_t' in namespace 'std'; did you mean simply 'ptrdiff_t'?`

Please, find the attached logs with full details.

Build","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Build related PR"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-09-09T07:41:24Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T14:17:15Z","closed_at":"2024-09-09T13:41:26Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27361","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27361","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27361.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27361.patch","merged_at":"2024-09-09T13:41:26Z"},"body":"This tiny PR fixes a failure happening when building numpy in C++23 language mode.

This issue is caused by the ongoing cleanup of C++ standard library from transitive includes, if they aren't explicitely allowed by the standard.

In particular, in [string_fastsearch.h](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/9e43697124aa7b854f6515b189233286f6a305d7/numpy/_core/src/umath/string_fastsearch.h#L132) we rely on `std::ptrdiff_t` but don't explicitely include its header, `cstddef`, as prescribed bt the [standard](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/ptrdiff_t).

Before C++23, this worked fine as some other headers we included transitvely included `cstddef`. 

Without this fix, both on Clang/LLVM and GCC upstreams the following error is observed:
`../numpy/_core/src/umath/string_fastsearch.h:132:5: error: no type named 'ptrdiff_t' in namespace 'std'; did you mean simply 'ptrdiff_t'?`

Please, find the attached logs with full details.

{"action":"opened","number":10677,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/OSGeo/gdal/pulls/10677","id":2044117795,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAF3Q3c551sMj","html_url":"https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/pull/10677","diff_url":"https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/pull/10677.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/pull/10677.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/OSGeo/gdal/issues/10677","number":10677,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"port/cpl_conv.cpp: Use the right header for std::endian in C++20/23","user":{"login":"gorloffslava","id":31761951,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxNzYxOTUx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/31761951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava","html_url":"https://github.com/gorloffslava","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gorloffslava/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## What does this PR do?

Currently, we import `std::endian` via `type_traits` header from C++ standard library.
[cpl_conv.cpp#L87](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/master/port/cpl_conv.cpp#L87)

However, based on [standard](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/endian), `std::endian` should be included via `bit` header.

`type_traits` worked fine with C++20, as it transitevely includes `bit` header. 
However, in C++23 standard forced headers to clean up transitive includes, leading to failures in compilation in C++23 mode.

We were able to reproduce that both with clang 18, 19git, 20git both with libc++ (LLVM) and libstdc++ (GNU GCC).

This tiny fix replaces `type_traits` header with `bit` one. I've tested that both in C++20 and C++23 language modes, and it solves the compilation error.

For information purposes, the original error has been attached in file. 
[logs.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/16800237/logs.txt)

Currently, [filegdbtable.cpp#L2734](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/master/ogr/ogrsf_frmts/openfilegdb/filegdbtable.cpp#L2734) uses `#if 0 ... #endif` construct to comment out the Python code snippet. 

In many cases it works fine, but with some sets of pre-processor defines/macros, it chokes on this snippet as trying to expand some parts of that (we weren't able to narrow down that to the minimal sets of flags as fixing that is simply easier).

This tiny PR replaces `#if 0 ... #endif` construct with usual C/C++ comments. It shouldn't/doesn't affect the code compilation where it worked previously but should prevent pre-processor from erroring in all cases there. 

Please, find the errors log snippet attached.

What does this PR do?

Currently, [filegdbtable.cpp#L2734](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/master/ogr/ogrsf_frmts/openfilegdb/filegdbtable.cpp#L2734) uses `#if 0 ... #endif` construct to comment out the Python code snippet. 

In many cases it works fine, but with some sets of pre-processor defines/macros, it chokes on this snippet as trying to expand some parts of that (we weren't able to narrow down that to the minimal sets of flags as fixing that is simply easier).

This tiny PR replaces `#if 0 ... #endif` construct with usual C/C++ comments. It shouldn't/doesn't affect the code compilation where it worked previously but should prevent pre-processor from erroring in all cases there. 

Please, find the errors log snippet attached.

Starting [[c26f7ea](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/commit/c26f7eabf4a1cd25353f22734f439505fe636a45)](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/commit/c26f7eabf4a1cd25353f22734f439505fe636a45), netcdf-c exposes the `NC_FillValue` macro instead of `_FillValue` to avoid collisions with [[C++ standard library](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/issues/2858)](https://github.com/Unidata/netcdf-c/issues/2858). However, the previous macro, `_FillValue`, was fully removed causing netcdf-c consumers, including GDAL, fail to build.

It's unlikely that this naming change will be backported to the previous netcdf-c releases, so we have to account for both macros variants. We do so by introducing our own macro, `NCDF_FillValue`, and using that in places where `_FillValue` was previously used. If `NC_FillValue` is defined by `netcdf.h`, `NCDF_FillValue` expands to it and, if it's not, to `_FillValue`.

I was able to successfully build the GDAL against netcdf-c both with:
1. `_FillValue`: v4.9.2 (from GitHub sources, Amazon Linux 2023's dnf, and macOS homebrew)
2. `NC_FillValue`: v4.9.3dev (the latest commit from the `main`, Given: 
- multi-file dataset (e.g., Shapefile) w/ one of the files having an uppercased extension (e.g., `.PRJ`)
- e.g., "poly" Shapefile dataset from the GDAL autotest data: https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/tree/master/autotest/ogr/data
- in particular, files: `poly.shp`, `poly.shx`, `poly.dbf`, `poly.PRJ`

Reproduction code:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds = gdal.OpenEx("poly.shp")
ds.GetFileList()
```

Expected behavior:
- on both case-sensitive and -insensitive file systems to get: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.PRJ']`

Actual behavior:
- case-sensitive filesystem: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.PRJ']`
- case-insensitive filesystem: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.prj']`

Extra note: 
- this only affects file extension, but not its name - if, for example, we'd rename all files in the dataset to `PoLy.*`, `GetFileList` would correctly output the following on both case-sensitive and -insensitive file systems: `['PoLy.shp', 'PoLy.shx', 'PoLy.dbf', 'PoLy.[PRJ|prj]']`.


## Operating system

The same behavior is observed on:
- macOS 14.0 beta 4; Amazon Linux 2, and Amazon Linux 2023
- the issue is not observed on case-sensitive file systems


## GDAL version and provenance

- GDAL 3.7.1 from conda-forge 
- self-compiled GDAL from `master` branch

## Steps to reproduce the problem.

Reproduction case #1:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds = gdal.OpenEx("testcsvt.csv")
ds.GetFileList()
```

Expected output: `['testcsvt.csv', 'testcsvt.csvt']`
Actual output: `['testcsvt.csv']`


Reproduction case #2:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds_src = gdal.OpenEx("testcsvt.csv")
driver = gdal.GetDriverByName("CSV")
ds_dst = driver.CreateCopy("test_csvt_copy", ds_src)

import os
os.listdir("test_csvt_copy")
```

Expected output: `['testcsvt.csv', 'testcsvt.csvt']`
Actual output: `['testcsvt.csv']`

Reproduction case #3:

```python
gdf.info()
```

Given:
- multi-file ESRI Shapefile dataset
- e.g., "poly" Shapefile dataset from the GDAL autotest data: https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/tree/master/autotest/ogr/data
- in particular, files: poly.shp, poly.shx, poly.dbf, poly.PRJ

Disclaimer:
- this doesn't relate to https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/issues/8164 - the issue persists despite we have `poly.prj` or `poly.PRJ`

Reproduction code:
```python
from osgeo import gdal

ds_src = gdal.OpenEx("poly.shp")
ds_src.GetFileList()
"""
['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.PRJ']
"""

driver = gdal.GetDriverByName("ESRI Shapefile")
ds_dst = driver.CreateCopy("test_shapefile", ds_src)
ds_dst.FlushCache()
ds_dst.GetFileList()
"""
['test_shapefile/poly.shp', 'test_shapefile/poly.shx', 'test_shapefile/poly.dbf', '']
```

Given: 
- multi-file dataset (e.g., Shapefile) w/ one of the files having an uppercased extension (e.g., `.PRJ`)
- e.g., "poly" Shapefile dataset from the GDAL autotest data: https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/tree/master/autotest/ogr/data
- in particular, files: `poly.shp`, `poly.shx`, `poly.dbf`, `poly.PRJ`

Reproduction code:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds = gdal.OpenEx("poly.shp")
ds.GetFileList()
```

Expected behavior:
- on both case-sensitive and -insensitive file systems to get: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.PRJ']`

Actual behavior:
- case-sensitive filesystem: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.PRJ']`
- case-insensitive filesystem: `['poly.shp', 'poly.shx', 'poly.dbf', 'poly.prj']`

Extra note: 
- this only affects file extension, but not its name - if, for example, we'd rename all files in the dataset to `PoLy.*`, `GetFileList` would correctly output the following on both case-sensitive and -insensitive file systems: `['PoLy.shp', 'PoLy.shx', 'PoLy.dbf', 'PoLy.[PRJ|prj]']`.

- Amazon Linux 2 (x86_64, Intel + aarch64, AWS Graviton2)
- Amazon Linux 2023 (x86_64, Intel + aarch64, AWS Graviton3E)
 
Tthe issue is not observed on case-sensitive file systems

> 
> > Naming Conventions
> > All file names adhere to the 8.3 naming convention. The main file, the index file, and the
> > dBASE file have the same prefix. The prefix must start with an alphanumeric character
> > (a–Z, 0–9), followed by zero or up to seven characters (a–Z, 0–9, _, -). The suffix for
> > the main file is .shp. The suffix for the index file is .shx. The suffix for the dBASE table
> > is .dbf. All letters in a file name are in lower case on operating systems with case
> > sensitive file names.
>

Expected behavior and actual behavior.

Given:
- CSV dataset w/ both `.csv` index and `.csvt` sidecar. 
- For example, `testcsvt.csv` and `testcsvt.csvt` from GDAL autotest data: https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/tree/master/autotest/ogr/data/csv

## Steps to reproduce the problem.

Reproduction case #1:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds = gdal.OpenEx("testcsvt.csv")
ds.GetFileList()
```

Expected output: `['testcsvt.csv', 'testcsvt.csvt']`
Actual output: `['testcsvt.csv']`


Reproduction case #2:
```python
from osgeo import gdal
ds_src = gdal.OpenEx("testcsvt.csv")
driver = gdal.GetDriverByName("CSV")
ds_dst = driver.CreateCopy("test_csvt_copy", ds_src)
ds_dst.FlushCache()

import os
os.listdir("test_csvt_copy")
```

Expected output: `['testcsvt.csv', 'testcsvt.csvt']`
Actual output: `['testcsvt.csv']`

Reproduction case #3: 
How can we checked that CSVT is really loaded by GDAL?
```python
import geopandas

gdf = geopandas.read_file("testcsvt.csv")
gdf.info()
"""
<class 'geopandas.geodataframe.GeoDataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 2 entries, 0 to 1
Data columns (total 10 columns):
 #   Column      Non-Null Count  Dtype         
---  ------      --------------  -----         
 0   INTCOL      1 non-null      float64       
 1   REALCOL     1 non-null      float64       
 2   STRINGCOL   2 non-null      object        
 3   INTCOL2     1 non-null      float64       
 4   REALCOL2    1 non-null      float64       
 5   STRINGCOL2  2 non-null      object        
 6   DATETIME    1 non-null      datetime64[ns]
 7   DATE        1 non-null      object        
 8   TIME        1 non-null      object        
 9   geometry    0 non-null      geometry      
dtypes: datetime64[ns](1), float64(4), geometry(1), object(4)
memory usage: 288.0+ bytes
"""
# Typings are applied correctly

os.remove("testcsvt.csvt")
gdf = geopandas.read_file("testcsvt.csv")
gdf.info()
"""
<class 'geopandas.geodataframe.GeoDataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 2 entries, 0 to 1
Data columns (total 10 columns):
 #   Column      Non-Null Count  Dtype   
---  ------      --------------  -----   
 0   INTCOL      2 non-null      object  
 1   REALCOL     2 non-null      object  
 2   STRINGCOL   2 non-null      object  
 3   INTCOL2     2 non-null      object  
 4   REALCOL2    2 non-null      object  
 5   STRINGCOL2  2 non-null      object  
 6   DATETIME    2 non-null      object  
 7   DATE        2 non-null      object  
 8   TIME        2 non-null      object  
 9   geometry    0 non-null      geometry
dtypes: geometry(1), object(9)
memory usage: 288.0+ bytes
"""
# Typings no longer work. Expected, as we deleted `.csvt` sidecar w/ them.
```

## Operating system

Reproducible w/ any of the following:
- macOS 14.0 beta 4 (x86_64, Intel)
- Amazon Linux 2 (x86_64, Intel + aarch64, AWS Graviton2)
- Amazon Linux 2023 (x86_64, Intel + aarch64, AWS Graviton3E)

## GDAL version and provenance

Reproducible w/ any of the following:
- GDAL 3.7.1 from conda-forge
- Self-compiled GDAL 3.8.0dev from `master` branch
